original_hypothesis.title: Factory removePool can revert or remove wrong pool if poolIndex/userPoolIndex are stale after swaps in arrays (operator error causes registry corruption/DoS)
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: Factory.removePool performs swap-and-pop on both the global pair pool list and the creator pool list, but requires caller-supplied indexes. If indexes are stale (due to prior removals/additions), the function may revert or remove an unintended pool from `pools[base][quote]`, causing registry inconsistency that can mislead UIs and integrators relying on Factory registry.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Not directly exploitable by unprivileged attackers (onlyOwner), but practical operational mistakes are common. Since Router/UX may rely on Factory registry for discovery/validation, registry corruption can lead to users interacting with wrong pools, losing funds due to pool selection errors (even without mempool attacks).
deployment_assumptions: Integrators/UIs rely on Factory.pools/userPools for pool discovery and trust its correctness
Owner operations may use off-chain indexing that can become stale
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: Factory.sol removePool requires poolIndex and userPoolIndex supplied externally; no bounds checks besides implicit array access and userPool equality check.

inventory_refs.function: removePool(address creator,address baseToken,address quoteToken,uint256 poolIndex,uint256 userPoolIndex)

proof_of_signal.pattern: swap/pop arrays using provided indices; only verifies _userPools[userPoolIndex] == pool

preconditions: Factory owner calls removePool with incorrect poolIndex (stale or wrong pair)
Registry consumers assume Factory.pools accurately lists valid pools

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: index-based deletion without verifying pool at poolIndex matches intended (beyond using it as reference)
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: registry may be corrupted or removals may revert unexpectedly
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: explicit bounds checks and/or pool address parameter verification in both arrays

analysis_questions: Is Factory registry used as a security boundary (e.g., Router validation) anywhere else in the codebase?
Are there scripts/admin tools that compute indices safely, or could they drift?
validation_requirements: Review any off-chain/indexing scripts and admin procedures
Add tests for multiple adds/removes ensuring consistent registry state
Consider safer removal API (remove by pool address with search or mapping indexes)

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Owner mistakenly removes the wrong pool from a token pair list, leaving the intended malicious or deprecated pool still registered. UIs route users to the wrong pool, causing unexpected pricing or losses.
prerequisites: Owner action error or compromised admin operations (off-chain)

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Indirect but potentially significant via user misrouting to wrong pools
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Users relying on Factory registry for pool selection
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Recoverable by registry correction, but damage may already occur

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: Likely minimal tests around registry mutation correctness under repeated removals

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Compare on-chain pool states vs UI/indexer listings after admin removals

validation_result.impact_summary: Filtered before validation: attack_realism is LOW
validation_result.detailed_analysis: This hypothesis was auto-rejected because its attack_realism grade is LOW. Preconditions require actions outside the attacker's control (off-chain compromise, victim cooperation, or admin collusion). No LLM validation was performed.
